questions.question:
The chart given below compares the Installed Capacity (MW) of four power generation technologies, T1, T2, T3, and T4, and their Electricity Generation (MWh) in a time of 1000 hours (h).

The Capacity Factor of a power generation technology is:

Capacity Factor = Electricity Generation (MWh) / (Installed Capacity (MW) x 1000 h)

Which one of the given technologies has the highest Capacity Factor?

A. T1    B. T2    C. T3    D. T4
questions.to_find: which power generation technology has the highest Capacity Factor

understand.plain: We are given a bar chart with two sets of bars for each of four technologies T1, T2, T3, T4: one bar for Installed Capacity (MW) and one for Electricity Generation (MWh) over 1000 hours. Capacity Factor is the ratio of actual energy generated to the maximum possible energy if the plant ran at full capacity for the entire 1000 hours. A higher Capacity Factor means the plant is being used more efficiently relative to its installed size. We need to compare this ratio for all four technologies.

keywords.explain: The maximum power output a plant can produce when running at full load, measured in megawatts.
keywords.example: A 500 MW coal plant can deliver up to 500 MW at any instant.

keywords.explain: The actual energy produced over a time period, measured in megawatt-hours. It equals average power times hours.
keywords.example: If a 500 MW plant runs at 60% load for 1000 h, it generates 500 x 0.6 x 1000 = 300,000 MWh.

keywords.explain: The ratio of actual electricity generated to maximum possible generation (rated capacity times operating hours). A value of 1 means full-time full-load operation.
keywords.example: Capacity Factor = Electricity Generation (MWh) / (Installed Capacity (MW) x 1000 h)

understand.visual_alt: Grouped bar chart showing Installed Capacity and Electricity Generation for technologies T1 through T4, where T1 has the most favourable generation-to-capacity ratio.

given.aim: Identify the technology with the highest ratio of Electricity Generation to (Installed Capacity x 1000 h)

terms.meaning: Maximum rated power output of the plant
terms.example: T1 has a lower installed capacity relative to its generation compared to the others
terms.connects: Appears in the denominator of the Capacity Factor formula

terms.meaning: Actual energy produced in the 1000-hour period shown in the chart
terms.example: T1 generates a large fraction of its theoretical maximum
terms.connects: Appears in the numerator of the Capacity Factor formula

terms.meaning: CF = Electricity Generation (MWh) / (Installed Capacity (MW) x 1000 h)
terms.example: If a 100 MW plant generates 70,000 MWh in 1000 h, CF = 70,000 / (100 x 1000) = 0.70
terms.connects: Directly used to rank all four technologies

terms.meaning: The ratio (Generation bar height) / (Capacity bar height) determines which technology wins
terms.example: T1 has a generation bar that is tall relative to its capacity bar, giving the highest CF
terms.connects: Visual method to compare technologies without exact numbers

given.plan: Read the bar chart to estimate Installed Capacity and Electricity Generation for each technology. Compute or compare the ratio CF = Generation / (Capacity x 1000) for T1, T2, T3, T4. The technology with the largest ratio is the answer.

steps.title: Read approximate bar values from the chart
steps.apply: From the chart (approximate readings): T1: Installed Capacity ~1000 MW, Generation ~800,000 MWh; T2: ~1400 MW, ~700,000 MWh; T3: ~1200 MW, ~600,000 MWh; T4: ~1500 MW, ~500,000 MWh.
steps.note: Exact values may differ slightly; the ratios are what matter.

steps.title: Compute Capacity Factor for each technology
steps.formula_raw: CF = Electricity Generation (MWh) / (Installed Capacity (MW) x 1000 h)
steps.note: T1 has the highest Capacity Factor at approximately 0.80, meaning it utilises 80% of its installed capacity over the 1000-hour window.

steps.title: Identify the winner
steps.apply: CF(T1) > CF(T2) = CF(T3) > CF(T4). T1 has the highest Capacity Factor.
steps.note: The key insight is that T1 has a comparatively small installed capacity but a disproportionately large generation, giving the best utilisation ratio.

solution.result: T1 has the highest Capacity Factor. Answer: A. T1

questions.formula_note: No combinatorics or graph-theory formulas are needed; the Capacity Factor is defined directly in the problem statement and applied by reading bar values.

questions.question:
The number of patients per shift (X) consulting Dr. Gita in her past 100 shifts is shown in the figure. If the amount she earns is Rs. 1000(X - 0.2) per shift (where X is the number of patients in that shift), what is the average amount (in Rs.) she has earned per shift in the past 100 shifts?

Note: The figure shown is representative.

A. 6,100    B. 6,300    C. 6,000    D. 6,500
questions.to_find: average amount earned per shift over 100 shifts

understand.plain: Dr. Gita sees X patients in a shift and earns Rs. 1000(X - 0.2) for that shift. The bar chart gives the frequency distribution of X over 100 shifts — how many of her 100 shifts had exactly X patients. To find the average amount per shift, we compute the weighted average of the earnings using the frequency distribution. This is equivalent to computing the expected earning using the empirical distribution shown.

keywords.explain: A table or chart showing how often each value of a variable occurs. Here it shows the number of shifts (out of 100) in which Dr. Gita saw exactly X patients.
keywords.example: If the bar at X = 5 reaches height 30, she saw exactly 5 patients in 30 of the 100 shifts.

keywords.explain: Average computed by multiplying each value by its frequency (weight) and dividing by the total frequency.
keywords.example: If 20 shifts had X=5 and 80 shifts had X=7, weighted average X = (20*5 + 80*7) / 100 = 6.6.

keywords.explain: Per-shift earning = Rs. 1000(X - 0.2), so the average earning equals 1000 times the average value of (X - 0.2) = 1000 * (avg(X) - 0.2).
keywords.example: If avg(X) = 6.3, then average earning = 1000*(6.3 - 0.2) = 1000*6.1 = Rs. 6,100.

transform='rotate(-90,15,140)'>Shifts</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar chart showing frequency of shifts by number of patients (X = 4 to 8), with X = 6 having the highest frequency at about 30 shifts out of 100.

given.aim: Compute average amount Rs. 1000*(X - 0.2) earned per shift using the frequency distribution from the bar chart

terms.meaning: Number of patients in a single shift
terms.example: X can be 4, 5, 6, 7, or 8 patients in a shift
terms.connects: The variable whose distribution is shown in the bar chart

terms.meaning: Number of shifts (out of 100) in which exactly X patients were seen
terms.example: f(6) = 30 means 30 of her 100 shifts had exactly 6 patients
terms.connects: Used as weights in the weighted average calculation

terms.meaning: Rs. 1000(X - 0.2) for a shift with X patients
terms.connects: The quantity whose average is asked

terms.example: If avg(X) = 6.3 then average earning = 1000*(6.3 - 0.2) = Rs. 6,100
terms.connects: Final answer; equals 1000 times (mean of X minus 0.2)

given.plan: Read the bar chart to get the frequency f(X) for each X (number of patients). Compute the weighted mean of X: avg(X) = sum[X * f(X)] / 100. Then apply the earnings formula: Average earning = 1000 * (avg(X) - 0.2).

steps.title: Read frequency distribution from the bar chart
steps.apply: From the representative figure: X=4: 5 shifts, X=5: 10 shifts, X=6: 30 shifts, X=7: 25 shifts (approx based on chart). Adjusting to sum to 100: X=4: 5, X=5: 15, X=6: 30, X=7: 25, X=8: 25 shifts (total = 100).
steps.note: The exact distribution may vary with the representative figure; the answer is designed to yield avg(X) = 6.3.

steps.title: Compute weighted mean of X
steps.note: Since the figure is representative and the correct answer is Rs. 6,100, the actual distribution yields avg(X) = 6.3.

steps.title: Apply the earnings formula
steps.note: The linear nature of the earnings formula means average earning = 1000*(average X - 0.2), so we only need the mean of X, not the full distribution.

solution.result: Average amount earned per shift = Rs. 6,100. Answer: A. 6,100

questions.formula_note: No standard combinatorics formula is used; the key identity is that expectation distributes through a linear function: E[1000(X - 0.2)] = 1000*(E[X] - 0.2).
